meta.title: The Game Class
meta.chapterTopics: Multiple classes, object interaction, encapsulated state

overview.body: One class is a container. Multiple classes working together is architecture. This week you add two more pieces to your program. First, the Player class: an object that knows where the player is, how far they have traveled, and what cities they have visited. Second, a Game class skeleton that holds the cities dictionary and the player, and can start a new game. You will wire them together in journey_test.py, which simulates a simple trip without a full game loop. Next week you complete the Game class and add inheritance. This week is about getting the objects talking to each other.

mentorQuote.quote: When classes work together, each one should do its own job and nothing else. The Player tracks the player. The City knows about the city. The Game coordinates between them. If you find yourself putting travel logic in the City class or city setup logic in the Player class, something is off.

objectives: Define a Player class that tracks name, location, miles, turns, and visited cities
Write Player methods for travel, stats accumulation, and route display
Define a Game class skeleton with city setup and player initialization
Create Player and City objects that interact through method calls
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 10: Program 10-9 (bankaccount2.py, methods that modify object state) and Program 10-10 (account_test2.py, creating objects and calling methods in sequence). The Player class follows the same accumulator pattern as BankAccount.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapter 10
programRequirements: player.py defines the Player class with __init__, getter methods, travel_to(), add_turn(), add_miles(), and __str__
game.py defines the Game class with __init__ that sets up cities and __setup_cities() private method
journey_test.py creates a Game object and a Player object and simulates at least 3 travel steps
All files have documentation headers and all methods have docstrings
overview.additionalFilesNote: Submit player.py, city.py (from 5.1, unchanged), game.py, and journey_test.py. CodeGrade tests Player methods in isolation.
overview.filenameNote: Submit player.py, city.py, game.py, and journey_test.py this week.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep all four files. Module 5.3 completes game.py with the full game loop and adds WinCity and DeadEndCity subclasses to city.py.

rows.task: Define Player with __init__, getters, travel_to(), add_turn(), __str__
Define Game with __init__ and __setup_cities() (cities dict, no player yet)
Add player to Game, write start_new_game() and get_current_city()
Create Game, simulate travel, verify Player stats

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your finished files fit together. The [NEW] tags show what you are adding this week. city.py carries forward unchanged from 5.1.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- city.py (UNCHANGED from 5.1) ----
class City:  ...all methods as built...

# ---- player.py [NEW] ----
class Player:
│
│── __init__(name, starting_city='San Antonio')
│   self.__name           string
│   self.__current_city   string
│   self.__total_miles    int (starts 0)
│   self.__total_turns    int (starts 0)
│   self.__visited_cities list (starts with starting_city)
│   self.__unique_cities  set  (starts with starting_city)
│
│── GETTERS
│   get_name(), get_current_city()
│   get_total_miles(), get_total_turns()
│   get_visited_cities(), get_unique_count()
│
│── TRAVEL METHODS
│   travel_to(city_name, distance)   updates city, miles, lists/set
│   add_turn()                       increments __total_turns
│   add_miles(miles)                 adds to __total_miles
│
│── DISPLAY
│   get_route_string()   ' -> '.join(visited_cities)
│   __str__              'Name at City (X mi)'

# ---- game.py [NEW] ----
class Game:
│
│── __init__()
│   self.__cities = {}     dict of name -> City objects
│   self.__player = None   set later in start_new_game()
│   self.__setup_cities()  called in __init__
│
│── __setup_cities()   [private] creates City objects, adds connections
│── start_new_game()   gets player name, creates Player object
│── get_current_city() returns City object for player's location

# ---- journey_test.py [NEW] ----
from game import Game
main():
│── Create Game object (cities set up automatically)
│── Call start_new_game() to create Player
│── Simulate 3+ moves: call game.get_current_city(),
│   pick a destination, call player.travel_to()
│── Print player __str__ after each move
│── Print final route and stats

content.body: Create player.py. The Player class tracks everything about the player's journey: where they are, how far they have gone, how many turns have elapsed, and where they have been. All of this used to be scattered as global variables in your game loop. Now it lives in one object.

items: Add a documentation header. Define class Player: with a class docstring.

Write __init__(self, name, starting_city='San Antonio'). Initialize private attributes: self.__name, self.__current_city (set to starting_city), self.__total_miles = 0, self.__total_turns = 0. Also initialize self.__visited_cities = [starting_city] (a list, preserves duplicates) and self.__unique_cities = set() then immediately self.__unique_cities.add(starting_city).

Write getter methods: get_name(), get_current_city(), get_total_miles(), get_total_turns(), get_visited_cities() (return a copy with .copy()), and get_unique_count() (return len(self.__unique_cities)).
Write travel_to(self, city_name, distance): update self.__current_city, add distance to self.__total_miles, append city_name to self.__visited_cities, and call self.__unique_cities.add(city_name).
Write add_turn(self): increment self.__total_turns by 1.
Write add_miles(self, miles): add miles to self.__total_miles.
Write get_route_string(self): return ' -> '.join(self.__visited_cities).
Write __str__(self): return f'{self.__name} at {self.__current_city} ({self.__total_miles} mi)'.

references.description: The deposit() method adds to __balance using +=. travel_to() does the same thing: it updates __total_miles and __current_city just like deposit() updates __balance. The pattern is identical.

content.title: CP1 Complete
items: player.py exists with Player class, docstrings on all methods
travel_to() updates current_city, total_miles, visited_cities list, and unique_cities set
get_visited_cities() returns a copy, not the original list
__str__ returns 'Name at City (X mi)' format

content.body: Create game.py. The Game class is the orchestrator. It holds all the City objects and the Player, and it will eventually run the full game loop. This week you build the foundation: city setup and the ability to start a new game. The game loop itself comes in 5.3.

items: Add imports at the top: import random, import function_library, from city import City, from player import Player.
Define class Game: with a class docstring. Write __init__(self): initialize self.__cities = {}, self.__player = None, then call self.__setup_cities().

Write __setup_cities(self) as a private method (double underscore). Inside, create City objects for at least 5 cities: San Antonio (START), Houston (GATEWAY), Austin (CORRIDOR), Corpus Christi (WIN), and Laredo (DEAD_END). Use the taglines from your Module 4 game.

After creating the City objects, add connections between them using city_object.add_connection(name, distance). Use the same distances from your Module 4 game. Add at least 3 trivia questions to San Antonio and Houston using city_object.add_trivia(question, answer).
Store each city in self.__cities with the city name as the key: self.__cities['San Antonio'] = san_antonio.

content.title: Private Methods Use Double Underscore Too
content.body: Just like private attributes, private methods use the double-underscore prefix: def __setup_cities(self):. This prevents outside code from calling setup directly. The method is called once from __init__ and never needs to be called again.

content.title: CP2 Complete
items: game.py imports random, function_library, City, and Player
Game.__init__ initializes __cities and __player, then calls __setup_cities()
__setup_cities() creates at least 5 City objects with connections and trivia stored in __cities dict
Creating a Game object does not crash

content.body: Add two more methods to the Game class: start_new_game(), which creates the Player object, and get_current_city(), which returns the City object for wherever the player currently is. These are the two methods journey_test.py will use.

items: Write start_new_game(self): get the player's name with input() and .strip(). Create self.__player = Player(name, 'San Antonio'). Print a welcome message.
Write get_current_city(self): get the player's current city name with self.__player.get_current_city(), then return self.__cities[city_name]. This returns the actual City object, not just the name.
Write get_player(self): return self.__player. This lets journey_test.py access the Player object to call travel methods.

content.quote: get_current_city() returns a City object, not a string. That is the key difference from Module 4. Instead of looking up a name in a dictionary, you are handing back something that knows what to do with itself. The Game does not have to know how to display destinations. It just asks the City to do it.

content.title: CP3 Complete
items: start_new_game() creates a Player object stored in self.__player
get_current_city() returns a City object (not a string)
get_player() returns the Player object

content.body: Create journey_test.py. This simulates a short trip using your Game and Player classes together. There is no game loop yet. You will manually call travel steps to verify everything is wired up correctly.

items: Add imports: from game import Game.
Write main(). Create a Game object. Call game.start_new_game().
Get the current city object: city = game.get_current_city(). Print it and call city.display_destinations().
Simulate travel: call game.get_player().travel_to('Houston', 200). Print the player object to verify the updated state.
Simulate two more travel steps to different cities. After each one, print the player and verify the city name and mileage update.
After all three steps, print the player's full route using game.get_player().get_route_string() and the unique city count using game.get_player().get_unique_count().
Add the if __name__ == '__main__': guard and call main().

content.title: Hardcoded Travel in the Test Is Fine
content.body: In journey_test.py, calling game.get_player().travel_to('Houston', 200) directly is correct. This is a test program. You are verifying the objects work, not simulating real gameplay. The real game loop with user input comes in 5.3.

content.title: Lab 5.2 Complete
items: player.py, city.py, game.py, and journey_test.py all submitted to CodeGrade
journey_test.py simulates at least 3 travel steps and prints player state after each
get_route_string() shows the full path including starting city
get_unique_count() is lower than len(visited_cities) if any city was visited twice
Documentation headers and docstrings present in all files

beforeYouSubmit: player.py: Player class with __init__, getters, travel_to(), add_turn(), add_miles(), get_route_string(), __str__
game.py: Game class with __init__, private __setup_cities(), start_new_game(), get_current_city(), get_player()
city.py: unchanged from 5.1, submitted alongside the new files
journey_test.py: creates Game, starts game, simulates 3+ travel steps, prints route and unique count
travel_to() updates both visited_cities list AND unique_cities set
get_visited_cities() returns a copy, not the original list
All files have documentation headers and all methods have docstrings
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 10 techniques only this week. Inheritance (Chapter 11) is reserved for 5.3. Do not create WinCity or DeadEndCity yet.

specific: Multiple class definitions across multiple files
Method returning an object: return self.__cities[name]
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list comprehensions, inheritance, WinCity or DeadEndCity subclasses (Module 5.3)

warnings: CodeGrade imports player.py directly and tests Player methods in isolation. Make sure Player works without a Game object.
function_library.py must be in the same directory as your submitted files. Submit it along with the others.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep all four files. Module 5.3 adds to city.py (inheritance) and completes game.py (full game loop, trivia gate, hazards, final report).

ifYouGetStuck: If importing Game crashes with a ModuleNotFoundError, verify city.py and player.py are in the same folder as game.py
If get_current_city() returns a string instead of an object, check that you are returning self.__cities[city_name] and not city_name itself
Re-read Program 10-10 (account_test2.py). It shows the same pattern of creating an object and calling methods on it in sequence
Submit to CodeGrade early to isolate which class is failing
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 10 BookEx Reference Guide, which covers Programs 10-9 and 10-10 showing the method-updating-state pattern that Player uses
